annotations.label: Numerical integration

anchor.snippet: comprises a broad family of algorithms for calculating the numerical value of a definite integral

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes exact integration (intervalIntegral) and one quadrature rule (trapezoidal), but has no general framework for numerical integration algorithms.

annotations.label: Numerical quadrature

anchor.snippet: The term numerical quadrature

annotations.note: The term/concept of numerical quadrature is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cubature

anchor.snippet: numerical integration over more than one dimension as cubature

annotations.note: Multidimensional numerical integration (cubature) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Basic problem of numerical integration

anchor.snippet: The basic problem in numerical integration is to compute an approximate solution to a definite integral

annotations.note: The definite integral object being approximated is formalized as intervalIntegral, but the approximation problem itself is not stated.

annotations.label: Non-elementary antiderivative (Gaussian)

anchor.snippet: An example of such an integrand is

annotations.note: integral_gaussian gives the value ∫ exp(-b·x²) = √(π/b), but the claim that exp(-x²) has no elementary antiderivative is not formalized.

annotations.label: Geometric mean construction for rectangle quadrature

anchor.snippet: equals their geometric mean

annotations.note: This classical compass-and-straightedge construction equating a rectangle's area to a square of side the geometric mean is not formalized.

annotations.label: Surface area of a sphere (Archimedes)

anchor.snippet: The area of the surface of a sphere is equal to quadruple the area of a great circle of this sphere

annotations.note: Mathlib has volume-of-balls results but no theorem giving the surface area of a sphere as 4πr².

annotations.label: Quadrature of the parabola (Archimedes)

anchor.snippet: The area of a segment of the parabola cut from it by a straight line is 4/3 the area of the triangle inscribed in this segment

annotations.note: No formalization of Archimedes' quadrature of the parabola exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Impossibility of squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: The quadrature of the circle with compass and straightedge had been proved in the 19th century to be impossible

annotations.note: Only the analytical part of Lindemann's theorem is present; transcendence of π (hence impossibility of squaring the circle) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Quadrature rule

anchor.snippet: A quadrature rule is an approximation of the definite integral of a function

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general definition of a quadrature rule as a weighted sum of function values; only the specific trapezoidal rule exists.

annotations.label: Midpoint / rectangle rule

anchor.section: Quadrature rules based on step functions
anchor.snippet: This is called the midpoint rule or rectangle rule

annotations.note: Mathlib has a related single-interval trapezoidal formula (trapezoidal_integral_one) but no genuine midpoint/rectangle rule using the midpoint value.

anchors.section: Quadrature rules based on step functions
anchors.snippet: This is called the midpoint rule or rectangle rule

annotations.label: Trapezoidal rule

anchor.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchor.snippet: This is called the trapezoidal rule

annotations.note: trapezoidal_integral defines integration of f over [a,b] using N trapezoids, exactly matching the trapezoidal rule.

anchors.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchors.snippet: This is called the trapezoidal rule

annotations.label: Composite rule

anchor.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchor.snippet: This is called a composite rule

annotations.note: The composite trapezoidal rule (sum over adjacent subintervals) is formalized, but the general notion of a composite rule for arbitrary base rules is not.

anchors.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchors.snippet: This is called a composite rule

annotations.label: Newton–Cotes formulas

anchor.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchor.snippet: yields the Newton–Cotes formulas

annotations.note: The trapezoidal rule is the two-point closed Newton–Cotes formula, but the general Newton–Cotes family is not formalized.

annotations.label: Simpson's rule

anchor.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchor.snippet: which is based on a polynomial of order 2

annotations.note: Simpson's rule (quadratic-interpolation quadrature) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gaussian quadrature accuracy

anchor.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchor.snippet: A Gaussian quadrature rule is typically more accurate than a Newton–Cotes rule

annotations.note: Gaussian quadrature and its accuracy/degree-of-exactness results are not formalized.

annotations.label: Clenshaw–Curtis quadrature

anchor.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchor.snippet: Other quadrature methods with varying intervals include Clenshaw–Curtis quadrature

annotations.note: Clenshaw–Curtis quadrature is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nesting of Gaussian and Gauss–Kronrod rules

anchor.section: Quadrature rules based on interpolating functions
anchor.snippet: Gaussian quadrature rules do not nest, but the related Gauss–Kronrod quadrature formulas do

annotations.note: Gauss–Kronrod rules and the nesting property are not formalized.

annotations.label: Adaptive quadrature

anchor.snippet: Adaptive quadrature is a numerical integration method

annotations.note: Adaptive quadrature algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extrapolation methods

anchor.snippet: extrapolating the result from two or more nonzero step sizes

annotations.note: Richardson-style extrapolation methods for integration are not formalized.

annotations.label: A priori error bound

anchor.snippet: our error is no greater than the right hand side

annotations.note: The supporting bound |∫_a^b g| ≤ (b−a)·sup‖g‖ is formalized, but the a priori quadrature error estimate is not stated as such.

anchors.snippet: our error is no greater than the right hand side

annotations.label: Riemann sum error bound

anchor.snippet: We can convert this into an error analysis for the Riemann sum

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Riemann-sum construction or associated error bound (its integral is the Bochner integral).

anchors.snippet: We can convert this into an error analysis for the Riemann sum

annotations.label: Quadrature rules for infinite intervals

anchor.snippet: The standard technique involves specially derived quadrature rules

annotations.note: Specially-derived quadrature rules for infinite intervals (e.g. Gauss–Hermite) are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: The standard technique involves specially derived quadrature rules

annotations.label: Tensor product rule via Fubini's theorem

anchor.snippet: phrase the multiple integral as repeated one-dimensional integrals by applying Fubini's theorem

annotations.note: integral_prod is exactly Fubini's theorem, expressing a product-measure integral as repeated one-dimensional integrals.

annotations.label: Curse of dimensionality

anchor.snippet: requires the function evaluations to grow exponentially as the number of dimensions increases

annotations.note: The exponential growth of work with dimension (curse of dimensionality) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Monte Carlo methods

anchor.snippet: Monte Carlo methods and quasi-Monte Carlo methods are easy to apply to multi-dimensional integrals

annotations.note: Monte Carlo / quasi-Monte Carlo integration methods are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Markov chain Monte Carlo

anchor.snippet: A large class of useful Monte Carlo methods are the so-called Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithms

annotations.note: Although Markov kernels exist in Mathlib's probability library, MCMC integration algorithms are not formalized.

annotations.label: Sparse grids (Smolyak)

anchor.snippet: Sparse grids were originally developed by Smolyak for the quadrature of high-dimensional functions

annotations.note: Smolyak sparse-grid quadrature is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bayesian quadrature

anchor.snippet: Bayesian quadrature is a statistical approach to the numerical problem of computing integrals

annotations.note: Bayesian quadrature is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduction of integral to an initial value problem

anchor.snippet: can be reduced to an initial value problem for an ordinary differential equation

annotations.note: The fundamental theorem of calculus (y(u)=∫_a^u f has derivative f) provides the mathematical content of this reduction, but it is not framed as reducing quadrature to an IVP.

annotations.label: Special form of the resulting differential equation

anchor.snippet: the right-hand side contains only the independent variable

annotations.note: The observation that the resulting ODE y'=f(x) has a right-hand side independent of y is not formalized as a statement.

annotations.label: RK4 yields Simpson's rule

anchor.snippet: the standard fourth-order Runge–Kutta method applied to the differential equation yields Simpson's rule

annotations.note: Neither the RK4 method nor Simpson's rule is formalized, so their equivalence is not stated.

annotations.label: Solving by quadrature

anchor.snippet: means expressing its solution in terms of integrals

annotations.note: The notion of solving a differential equation 'by quadrature' is not defined in Mathlib.
